title: Luke 17
subtitle: Sin, Faith, Gratitude, and the Coming Kingdom

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — Causes of Stumbling; Forgiveness; Faith Like a Mustard Seed

heb.paragraph: The word (v.1) originally described the trigger of a trap — the stick that, when touched, snapped the snare shut. Applied to human behaviour, it describes anything that trips another person into sin. Jesus treats causing another to stumble as a capital offence worthy of millstone-and-sea.

heb.gloss: faith the size of a mustard seed
heb.paragraph: The comparative (v.6) is not about quantity of faith but quality — the genuine article, however small. The mustard seed was proverbially the smallest agricultural seed. The point: even the tiniest genuine faith can do the impossible, because faith connects the one who has it to the God who does the impossible.

places.name: Samaria-Galilee Border
places.text: The frontier between the territories of Samaria and Galilee in the first century — a social as well as geographic boundary. Jesus's route through this region on his way to Jerusalem (9:51) is one of Luke's periodic geographical reminders that the journey continues. The border setting makes the Samaritan leper's presence in the group of ten plausible.

refs.note: Millstone and stumbling blocks — parallel warning in Matthew's community discourse.

"Truly I tell you, if you have faith as small as a mustard seed, you can say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it will move" — Matthew's version uses a mountain rather than a mulberry tree.
Naaman the Syrian healed of leprosy by Elisha — the OT precedent for a foreigner receiving cleansing that Israelites failed to seek, which Jesus references in 4:27.

notes.note: "If they repent, forgive them. Even if they sin against you seven times in a day..." — the repetition is the point. Forgiveness is not a one-time act but a continuous disposition. Seven times in a day makes the command feel impossible — which is precisely why the apostles immediately respond "Increase our faith!" The demand for repeated forgiveness is the context for the faith request.

The mustard-seed faith saying is not an invitation to attempt botanical miracles but an assurance that even tiny genuine faith is sufficient for the demands Jesus places on his disciples — including the demand for repeated forgiveness just stated. The "mulberry tree" (sykaminon) is specifically a deep-rooted tree, making the transplantation image even more striking.

"Unworthy servants; we have only done our duty" — this corrects any theology of merit. Obedience does not put God in our debt; it simply fulfils what was expected. Grace cannot be earned by obedience because obedience is already owed. The servant who has done everything commanded has done nothing extraordinary; he has simply done what the relationship required.

The nine who did not return are not condemned — they followed Jesus's instruction to go to the priests. The Samaritan's return is extraordinary, not normative. But Jesus's question ("Where are the other nine? Has no one returned to give praise to God except this foreigner?") makes the contrast stark: the one who had least reason to return, by the standards of covenant belonging, was the one who returned.

Calvin uses the duty parable to counter any doctrine of merit or supererogation (works beyond what is required). If we obey completely, we have done only what the master was owed. There is no surplus of obedience that can be stored up or transferred. This is directed against both Protestant pride in good works and the Catholic doctrine that saints' surplus merits can be distributed to others.

"Your faith has made you well" — the same formula used at other healings (7:50; 8:48). The nine were healed by the same power; the Samaritan received something more — not just physical cleansing but the pronouncement of wholeness that Jesus alone could give. Faith here is not the mechanism of healing but the relationship that opens to the fuller gift.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The mulberry tree (sykaminos) was proverbially deep-rooted in antiquity — rabbinic sources mention it as lasting 600 years. The image of uprooting such a tree and planting it in the sea combines deep-rootedness with the impossibility of growing in saltwater. The point is not botany but the impossible made possible by genuine faith.

"As they went, they were cleansed" — the healing occurs during the journey of obedience, not at the moment of the command. This pattern — healing in the act of obedient response — appears also in the blind man at Siloam (John 9:7). Faith acts before the evidence is complete.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Anendekton — "impossible that" (literally "unable to be received/accepted"). The strongest possible expression of impossibility: skandala are inevitable because of human sinfulness, but woe to the one who provides them. The inevitability does not excuse the agent.

Sykaminō — a mulberry tree (not the black mulberry, which was also called sykaminos, but the white mulberry or possibly the sycamore-fig). Distinguished from the sykomōrea (sycamore) of 19:4. Whatever the tree, it is deep-rooted and therefore a striking image for the impossible.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: "If you forgive seven times, you have gone beyond ordinary human patience. But Christ says: and again, and yet again. For it is not the number of times that matters but the disposition — to be always ready to receive the repentant, however many times they return."

Bede: "He was a Samaritan — that is, a foreigner and one rejected by the Jews. Yet he alone returned to give thanks. So often those who are far from the chosen community give back to God what the chosen keep for themselves."

hist.context:
Boundary Between Samaria and Galilee
Jesus's route (v.11) through the border region between Samaria and Galilee is geographically specific and theologically significant. The border was a social and religious boundary — Jewish-Samaritan relations were hostile. The ten lepers' mixed company (nine Jews, presumably, and one Samaritan) suggests that shared suffering crossed the usual boundaries.

Leprosy and Social Exclusion
Biblical "leprosy" (tsara'at) covered a range of skin conditions requiring Levitical examination and social isolation (Lev 13-14). The requirement to "stand at a distance" (v.12) and the command to "show yourselves to the priests" (v.14) both reflect the Levitical purity system. The Samaritan's return to Jesus rather than a Jewish priest is itself an implicit theological statement.

sections.header: Verses 11–19 — The Ten Lepers: Where Are the Other Nine?

heb.paragraph: The debated phrase (v.21) is either "within you" (the kingdom is an interior spiritual reality) or "in your midst / among you" (the kingdom is present in Jesus's person and ministry). The latter is more consistent with Luke's usage elsewhere — the kingdom arrives in events and persons, not primarily as an interior state.

The comparison (v.24) emphasises simultaneity and universality: lightning lights up the whole sky at once; the Son of Man's day will be equally instantaneous and unmissable. No local announcement ("here he is!") will be needed; the event will be self-evident to all.

places.name: Samaria-Galilee Border
places.text: The frontier between the territories of Samaria and Galilee in the first century — a social as well as geographic boundary. Jesus's route through this region on his way to Jerusalem (9:51) is one of Luke's periodic geographical reminders that the journey continues. The border setting makes the Samaritan leper's presence in the group of ten plausible.

refs.note: Matthew's parallel eschatological teaching, placed in the Olivet Discourse after the triumphal entry rather than during the Galilean journey.
The days of Noah — the setting of the flood narrative that Jesus invokes as a type of the final judgment.
The destruction of Sodom and the escape of Lot — the second OT type Jesus uses, with the specific warning to remember Lot's wife (Gen 19:26).

notes.note: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" — the Pharisees are asking the wrong question. They want a calendar; Jesus offers a reality. The kingdom is already present in the one they are questioning. Their inability to see this is itself evidence of the kingdom's hiddenness from the self-sufficient.

The lightning simile corrects the false prophets who will say "here he is" or "there he is." The Son of Man's coming will require no announcement, no location, no searching. It will be as self-evident as lightning that illuminates the entire sky simultaneously. Seeking localised signs is therefore a category error about the nature of the event.

"Remember Lot's wife" — the shortest command in Luke, and one of the most ominous. Her sin was not curiosity but attachment: looking back at what she was leaving, she was destroyed by the very judgment she was escaping. Jesus applies it directly: when the day comes, do not go back for possessions (v.31). Attachment to the old life is incompatible with rescue.

The paradox of losing to save and saving to lose appears in multiple contexts in Luke (9:24; 14:26-27). In the eschatological context of 17:33, it means: those who try to preserve their earthly life at the cost of their response to the Son of Man will lose the only life that matters; those who lose their earthly life in faithfulness to him will preserve it.

Calvin takes entos hymōn as "among you" rather than "within you" — the kingdom is present in Christ himself, not in an inner spiritual experience. The Pharisees' error is demanding an external visible sign of what is already standing before them. Calvin uses this to argue against all attempts to locate the kingdom geographically or chronologically.

The days of Noah and Lot teach that the final judgment will come upon a world absorbed in ordinary life, not upon a world in obvious moral crisis. This is Calvin's point about the danger of prosperity: comfort breeds complacency, and complacency is the most dangerous spiritual condition because it feels like safety.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Greek entos has generated one of the most sustained debates in Lukan scholarship. Arguments for "within you": (1) the word normally means "within" in Greek; (2) it fits the interior kingdom theme. Arguments for "in your midst/among you": (1) Jesus would not say the kingdom is "within" the Pharisees who oppose him; (2) it fits Luke's consistent presentation of the kingdom as present in Jesus's ministry. Most modern commentators favour "among you."

Verse 36 ("Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left") is present in many MSS but absent from the best witnesses (p75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). NA28 brackets or omits it as a scribal addition harmonising with Matt 24:40.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Entos — the lexical debate is genuine. LSJ gives both "within" and "among" as valid meanings. Robertson himself favours "in the midst of you" (among you, in your presence) as more consistent with Luke's kingdom theology. The present tense (estin) suggests a current reality, not a future interior state.

"Where the body is, there the vultures will gather" — a proverb of inevitable consequence. Just as carrion attracts vultures without announcement, the coming of the Son of Man will be attracted by the moral condition of the earth. Robertson notes that the "eagles" of KJV is better rendered "vultures" (aetoi can mean either, but the context requires scavengers).

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Origen: "The kingdom of God is within us — that is, in our intellect and our soul — when we make progress and it advances in us. For the kingdom is not a place but a spiritual condition, and it grows in those who grow toward it."

Chrysostom: "In Noah's time, people were going about their ordinary business right up to the flood. The catastrophe came not because they were doing evil things but because they were absorbed in ordinary things to the exclusion of the things of God. This is the warning: ordinariness without awareness is its own kind of blindness."

hist.context:
When Does the Kingdom Come?
The Pharisees' question about the timing of the kingdom reflects a common apocalyptic expectation — a datable, observable arrival of the divine reign. Jesus rejects both the calculability (it is not something that can be observed, 17:20) and the locality (not "here" or "there," 17:21). The kingdom's presence is not a future event to be calculated but a present reality to be discerned in Jesus himself.

Noah and Lot as Eschatological Types
Both Noah (Gen 6-9) and Lot (Gen 18-19) are OT figures of divine rescue preceding divine judgment. In both cases, ordinary life continued up to the moment of catastrophe — eating, drinking, marrying, buying, selling. The lesson is not that these activities are wrong but that they created a false sense of security that blinded people to the crisis.

sections.header: Verses 20–37 — The Coming of the Kingdom; The Days of the Son of Man

ppl.name: Jesus
ppl.text: Luke 17 spans from community ethics (forgiveness, faith, duty) to miraculous healing to eschatological teaching — the range of Luke's Jesus in a single chapter.

ppl.name: The Ten Lepers
ppl.text: Nine Jews and one Samaritan, united by their condition and separated by their response. The nine fulfill the legal requirement (showing themselves to priests); the one returns with something beyond legal compliance — gratitude and faith.

ppl.name: The Samaritan Leper
ppl.text: Fourth Samaritan in Luke's positive portraits (cf. 10:33-37; 17:16; and implicitly Acts 8). The pattern is consistent: the outsider models what the insider should have been.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationentos hymōn (17:21)KJV: "within you"; NIV: "in your midst"; ESV: "in the midst of you"; NRSV: "among you". Modern translations generally favour "among/in your midst" against KJV's "within."aetoi (17:37)KJV: "eagles"; NIV/ESV/NRSV: "vultures". The Greek can mean either; context requires scavenging birds, making "vultures" correct.

src.title: Genesis 6-9
src.quote: The flood narrative — Noah's world continued in ordinary life until the rain began. Jesus uses it as a type of the sudden, unmissable arrival of the Son of Man's day.
src.note: The comparison is not about Noah's generation being peculiarly wicked but about their absorption in ordinary life to the exclusion of divine warning.

src.title: Genesis 19
src.quote: The destruction of Sodom and Lot's rescue — with the specific detail of Lot's wife looking back (19:26). Jesus's "Remember Lot's wife" (17:32) is the shortest eschatological warning in the Gospels.
src.note: Lot's wife represents attachment to the life being left behind — the same attachment Jesus warns against in 17:31 (going back for possessions).

rec.who: The Kingdom "Within You" in Mystical Theology
rec.text: The KJV rendering "the kingdom of God is within you" became the foundational text for Christian mystical and interior spirituality — from Meister Eckhart and Thomas à Kempis through Quaker inwardness to Tolstoy's The Kingdom of God Is Within You (1894). The "among you" reading, now favoured by most scholars, has been a corrective, but the interior reading has proved remarkably generative in the tradition.

The three words of 17:32 ("Remember Lot's wife") have echoed through Christian reflection on detachment, worldliness, and the cost of discipleship. They appear in monastic literature as a warning against nostalgia for the life renounced, and in Puritan writing as an example of the danger of half-conversion — those who begin the journey but look back at what they are leaving.

rows.label: Two Audiences — vv.20-21 / 22-37
rows.text: The chapter's eschatological teaching is addressed to two audiences: the Pharisees (17:20-21, about the observable kingdom) and the disciples (17:22-37, about the Son of Man's day). The teaching differs in both content and emphasis — the Pharisees get a present-tense statement; the disciples get future-tense warnings.

rows.label: Noah and Lot Parallelism — vv.26-29
rows.text: Two OT catastrophes presented in parallel construction: "just as it was in the days of Noah / it was the same in the days of Lot." Both list ordinary activities; both end with sudden destruction. The parallelism intensifies the warning through repetition.

hebtext.note: The word (v.1) originally described the trigger of a trap — the stick that, when touched, snapped the snare shut. Applied to human behaviour, it describes anything that trips another person into sin. Jesus treats causing another to stumble as a capital offence worthy of millstone-and-sea.

hebtext.note: The comparative (v.6) is not about quantity of faith but quality — the genuine article, however small. The mustard seed was proverbially the smallest agricultural seed. The point: even the tiniest genuine faith can do the impossible, because faith connects the one who has it to the God who does the impossible.

The debated phrase (v.21) is either "within you" (the kingdom is an interior spiritual reality) or "in your midst / among you" (the kingdom is present in Jesus's person and ministry). The latter is more consistent with Luke's usage elsewhere — the kingdom arrives in events and persons, not primarily as an interior state.

The comparison (v.24) emphasises simultaneity and universality: lightning lights up the whole sky at once; the Son of Man's day will be equally instantaneous and unmissable. No local announcement ("here he is!") will be needed; the event will be self-evident to all.

The word (v.1) originally described the trigger of a trap — the stick that, when touched, snapped the snare shut. Applied to human behaviour, it describes anything that trips another person into sin. Jesus treats causing another to stumble as a capital offence worthy of millstone-and-sea.

hebtext.gloss: faith the size of a mustard seed
hebtext.note: The comparative (v.6) is not about quantity of faith but quality — the genuine article, however small. The mustard seed was proverbially the smallest agricultural seed. The point: even the tiniest genuine faith can do the impossible, because faith connects the one who has it to the God who does the impossible.

tx.title: Verse 36 omitted
tx.content: NA28 omits "Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left" — absent from the best early witnesses (p75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and almost certainly a scribal addition harmonising with Matt 24:40.
tx.note: The omission preserves the original: Luke has the bed and the grinding mill; Matthew has the field. Both are authentic tradition; Luke's version did not originally include the field.

tx.title: entos — no textual variant
tx.content: The manuscript tradition is uniform on entos; the debate is purely interpretive. The NRSV and NIV translate "among you"; KJV "within you." No manuscript supports one reading over the other — this is a question of lexicography and context, not textual criticism.
tx.note: Context and Lukan usage strongly favour "among you." The lexical evidence for "within" in this context is weaker than the contextual evidence for "among."

debate.title: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?

positions.name: Within you (interior)
positions.argument: Entos normally means "within/inside." The kingdom is an interior spiritual reality present in the hearts of those who receive it. This reading shaped centuries of Christian spirituality.

positions.name: In your midst (among you)
positions.proponents: Most modern scholars: Fitzmyer, Green, Marshall, Bock
positions.argument: Jesus would not say the kingdom is "within" the Pharisees who oppose him. Luke consistently presents the kingdom as present in Jesus's ministry and person. Entos can mean "among" (Ps 109:30 LXX uses it this way).

themes.note: Luke 17 moves through three movements: the ethics of community life (forgiveness, faith, duty), a dramatic healing that exposes the paradox of gratitude (the foreigner returns; the nine do not), and eschatological teaching that reframes both present and future. The kingdom is already present in Jesus's midst; the Son of Man's coming will be unmissable; between now and then, the disciples are to forgive repeatedly, serve without expectation of reward, and hold earthly life loosely enough that when the moment comes, they will not look back.

coaching.tip: 'We are unworthy servants; we have only done our duty' (v. 10). After doing everything commanded, no merit accumulates. Obedience is baseline, not bonus. Grace, not wages.
'One of them, when he saw he was healed, came back, praising God... and he was a Samaritan' (vv. 15-16). Ten lepers healed; one returns—the outsider. 'Where are the other nine?' Gratitude is rare.
